text: The surgeon places ports in the left upper quadrant, aligning them in a straight line with eight centimeters spacing towards the right lower quadrant.

The transverse colon is sutured to the abdominal wall to free robotic instruments for dissection and exposure. The right colon is also sutured to the abdominal wall for proper suspension and exposure.
The surgeon opens the mesenteric envelope and dissects the tumors using a medial-lateral approach. The duodenum is exposed, and the ileocolic vessels are identified.
The surgeon clips and divides the ileocolic vein originating from the superior mesenteric vein. The distal ileum is identified, and the mesentery is divided to address adhesions in the pelvis.
The surgeon frees the small intestine, preparing it for anastomosis later in the operation.

With the robotic fourth arm providing proper exposure, the surgeon continues the dissection in a cephalad direction, identifying the right colic vein, which is then clipped and divided using robotic clips.
The surgeon identifies the right colic artery, clips it, and divides it, completing the separation of this vessel from surrounding tissues.
The surgical field shows the pancreas and duodenum in close proximity, with clear visualization of their anatomical relationship.
The surgeon continues dissection on top of the pancreas and duodenum, carefully separating tissue layers in this region.
The surgeon uses the fourth robotic arm to take down the suture, enhancing retraction and exposure in the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the right branch of the midcolic vessels, which is then clipped and divided.

The surgical team replaces a sponge underneath the transverse colon and pulls it caudally to enter the lesser sac. They divide the omentum and identify the right gastroepiploic vessels on the greater curvature of the stomach, which are then divided. The dissection progresses towards the hepatic flexure.
The surgeon attempts to identify and connect the spaces from the opposite side where the sponge was previously placed, focusing on the gastrocolic trunk.
The surgeon continues the dissection towards the hepatic flexure to fully mobilize the right colon, using the previously removed suture to alleviate tension during the procedure.
The surgeon divides the distal ileum using a surgical instrument, completing the separation of this section of the small intestine.
The surgeon marks the transverse colon with a suture and proceeds to divide it, continuing the dissection process.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to completely divide any remaining attachments of the right colon, ensuring full mobilization of the structure.
The surgeon has completed the disconnection of the colorectal structures and placed the specimen to the side, indicating the completion of the mobilization and dissection process.
The distal ileum is positioned close to the transverse colon, showing the spatial relationship between these two anatomical structures.
The surgeon parks the anastomosis in the right upper quadrant, placing a suture and using the fourth arm of the surgical robot for optimal exposure.
The surgeon demonstrates an isoperistaltic anastomosis, showing the alignment of the bowel segments to maintain peristaltic flow.
The surgeon performs a metacollotomy, likely involving an incision or division in the colon area.

The surgeon uses indocyanine green (ICG) to assess the blood supply of the small intestine and transverse colon. Following this, a 45mm stapler is prepared for creating the anastomosis between these structures.
The surgeon uses the fourth arm of the robotic system to align the isoperistaltic anastomosis between the distal ileum and transverse colon, ensuring proper positioning in the surgical field.
The surgeon closes the common channel opening using a 3-0 absorbable barbed suture, employing a canal suture technique with a nine-inch length suture.
The completed isoperistaltic anastomosis is shown, with the barbed suture locked on itself to secure the closure.
The surgeon uses indocyanine green (ICG) fluorescence to assess the blood supply of the constructed anastomosis, ensuring adequate perfusion.
The surgeon removes all sutures from the anastomosis site, confirming proper blood supply to the area.
The specimen is extracted through the stapler port, with four visible incisions on the abdominal surface.
